title: Will the Test Pass?
blurb: Read the method and the test, then predict the result. A test fails when the actual value doesn't match what you asserted — or errors out when the code blows up before it even gets there.

docs.label: Microsoft Learn — Unit testing C# with xUnit (hands-on)

levels.title: L1 · Read the method
levels.prompt: Does this test pass or fail?

blocks.label: Method under test
blocks.code: int Add(int a, int b) => a + b;

failStates.title: It passes
failStates.explain: Add(2, 3) returns 5, and the test asserts Equal(5, 5) — that's true, so the test is green.

levels.title: L2 · Expected vs actual
levels.prompt: Look closely at the operator. Pass or fail?

blocks.label: Method under test
blocks.code: int Add(int a, int b) => a - b;

failStates.title: The method has a bug
failStates.explain: Add uses a - b, so Add(2, 3) is -1, not 5. xUnit reports: Assert.Equal() Failure — Expected: 5, Actual: -1. That message is exactly how a real test catches this bug.

levels.title: L3 · When null comes back
levels.prompt: No user has id 999. What does this test actually do?

failStates.title: There's no user 999
failStates.explain: FirstOrDefault returns null when nothing matches, so user is null — there's nothing here that could pass.

failStates.title: It never reaches the comparison
failStates.explain: user is null, so user.Name throws NullReferenceException on that line — before Assert.Equal ever runs. The test errors out instead of failing cleanly. The right test asserts the null behavior on purpose: Assert.Null(user).
